title: Dog Eyelid Rolling Inward: Guide to Entropion and Eye Health
meta_desc: Is your dog's eyelid rolling inward? Learn about Entropion, the risks to your pet's vision, and why this condition requires a veterinary visit.
content_html:
What is Entropion?

When a dog's eyelid rolls inward so that the hair and lashes rub against the eye, the condition is medically known as entropion. This structural abnormality can cause chronic irritation, as the sensitive surface of the eye—the cornea—is constantly being abraded by the fur. It is commonly seen in specific breeds with loose skin or certain facial structures, but it can also occur due to scarring or persistent squinting from other eye issues.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for an inward-rolling eyelid is Medium. While it is not usually a life-threatening situation in the short term, it is extremely painful for your dog and can lead to serious complications. If left untreated, the constant rubbing can cause corneal ulcers, pigmentation, or even perforation of the eye, which may lead to permanent vision loss. You should aim to have your pet seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, close-up photo of your dog's eye to your veterinary team can be very helpful. A photo allows the professional to see the degree of the roll, the presence of any discharge, and whether the surface of the eye appears cloudy or damaged. This visual information helps the clinic prioritize your appointment and gives them a baseline to compare against during the physical examination.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery for an inward-rolling eyelid?
faqs.answer: In many cases, yes. Because entropion is a physical misalignment of the eyelid, surgery is often the most effective way to permanently correct the position and stop the irritation.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops to help my dog's eye?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications on your dog without consulting a vet. Some human drops contain ingredients that can be harmful or worsen a corneal ulcer.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of discomfort to look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for squinting, excessive blinking (blepharospasm), watery eyes, or your dog frequently pawing at their face and rubbing their eye against furniture.
